1: lāmmâ YHWH taʿămōd bĕrāḥôq (lāmmâ YHWH taʿămōḏ bĕrāḥôq): 'Why, O LORD, do you stand far off?' The acrostic continues from Psalm 9 (together covering ʾālep through kāp), though Psalm 10 lacks an explicit superscription in Hebrew — further evidence of original unity. The question lāmmâ ('why?') opens a lament of divine hiddenness: God's standing bĕrāḥôq ('at a distance') and hiding bĕʿittôt bĕṣārâ ('in times of trouble') contradicts the refuge he is declared to be in 9:9. The tension is deliberate: the psalmist prays out of experienced abandonment toward known theology.
2: bĕgaʾăwat rāšāʿ yiddalaqu ʿānî (bĕḡaʾăwaṯ rāšāʿ yiddalaqu ʿānî): 'in arrogance the wicked pursues the poor.' The verb dālaq ('pursue hotly, burn after') describes predatory intensity. The noun gaʾăwâ ('pride, arrogance') is the root evil: the wicked man's self-elevation drives his exploitation. The passive/reflexive prayer yitkĕṭĕšû bĕmaḥšĕbôt zû ḥāšābû, 'let them be caught in the schemes they have devised,' echoes the lex talionis principle established in ch.9 — the trap-set becomes the trap-caught.
3: kî hillēl rāšāʿ ʿal-taʾăwat napšô (kî hillēl rāšāʿ ʿal-taʾăwaṯ napšô): 'for the wicked praises himself for the desire of his soul.' The piel hillēl ('to praise, boast') — root of hallĕlûyāh — is here inverted: the wicked praises himself rather than YHWH, treating his own desires as ultimate. The phrase ûbōṣēaʿ bērēk niʾēṣ YHWH, 'and the one who grasps for gain blesses himself but despises the LORD,' displays the theological corruption of covetousness: greed simultaneously demands self-blessing and despises God. The noun bōṣēaʿ ('one who cuts off/gains by violence') implies criminal acquisition.
4: rāšāʿ kĕgōbah ʾappô bal-yidrōš (rāšāʿ kĕḡōḇah ʾappô ḇal-yiḏrōš): 'the wicked, in his haughtiness, does not seek.' The phrase ʾên ʾĕlōhîm kol-mĕzimmôtāyw, 'there is no God in all his schemes,' is not theoretical atheism but practical atheism: the wicked operates as though God neither sees nor judges. The noun mĕzimmôt ('plans, schemes') is morally neutral in itself but here describes a mind from which God has been systematically excluded. Paul draws on vv.7 and 14 (LXX: Pss 9–10 were one psalm in LXX) in his catena of universal sinfulness in Rom 3:10-18.
5: yāḥîlû dĕrākāyw bĕkol-ʿēt (yāḥîlû dĕrāḵāyw bĕḵol-ʿēṯ): 'his ways prosper at all times.' The hiphil yāḥîlû (from ḥûl, 'twist/writhe/succeed') may mean 'his ways are firm/stable.' The apparent prosperity of the wicked is the classic theodicy problem (cf. Ps 73). The phrase mārôm mišpāṭêkā miggĕdô, 'your judgments are on high, out of his sight,' means the wicked cannot perceive divine governance — his immediate success blinds him. He puffs contempt at his enemies: yāpîaḥ bāhem, 'he blows at them,' an image of arrogant dismissal.
6: ʾāmar bĕlibbô bal-ʾemmôṭ (ʾāmar bĕlibbô ḇal-ʾemôṭ): 'he said in his heart, 'I shall not be moved.'' The inner speech ʾāmar bĕlibbô ('he said in his heart') introduces the wicked man's self-deception. The boast bal-ʾemmôṭ lĕdōr wādōr ʾăšer lōʾ bĕrāʿ, 'I shall not be moved; throughout all generations I shall not meet adversity,' is a counterfeit of YHWH's own eternal stability (9:7). The wicked imitates divine permanence — precisely the Edenic temptation (Gen 3:5: 'you will be like God').
7: ʾālâ pîhû māʾēn ûmirmâ (ʾālâh pîhû māʾēn ûmirmâ): 'his mouth is full of cursing and deceit and oppression.' The noun ʾālâ ('oath, curse') refers to invoking divine curses against others; mirmâ is 'treachery/deceit'; tōk is 'oppression/fraud.' Three words capture the weaponization of speech. Paul cites a version of this verse in Rom 3:14 ('their mouth is full of cursing and bitterness') as evidence of universal sinfulness — a NT appropriation of what the psalmist observed in a specific oppressor to diagnose a universal human condition.
8: yēšēb bĕmaʾărab ḥăṣērîm (yēšēḇ bĕmaʾĂraḇ ḥăṣērîm): 'he sits in ambush in the villages.' The noun maʾărab ('ambush') describes premeditated predation; ḥăṣēr ('village, courtyard') is where the vulnerable live. The imagery intensifies: the wicked hides bĕmistārîm ('in hiding places') to murder nāqî, 'the innocent.' His eyes secretly watch (bĕmistārîm yeṣappĕnû ʿênāyw) for the helpless — surveillance used for predation rather than protection.
9: yeʾĕrōb bammistār kĕʾaryēh bĕsukkô (yeʾĕrōḇ ḇammistār kĕʾaryēh bĕsukkô): 'he lies in wait in secret like a lion in its den.' The lion simile (kĕʾaryēh) is the Psalter's stock image for a predator who stalks rather than fights openly. The victim is ʿānî ('the poor/afflicted'), the same social group the psalm defends. The verb yimšōk ('he drags/draws away') — used of dragging a net catch — captures the violent, irreversible nature of the oppression. The poor man is prey.
10: yiddakkeh yāšōaḥ wĕnāpal (yiddakkeh yāšōaḥ wĕnāpal): 'he crouches, he stoops, and the poor man falls.' The sequence of three verbs — yiddakkeh (crushed down), yāšōaḥ (stoops), nāpal (falls) — creates a cinematic slow-motion of the predator's approach and the victim's collapse. The phrase bĕḥēlĕkāyw ḥêlekā ʿăniyyîm, 'by his mighty ones the poor are crushed,' suggests the wicked man's network of power — he does not act alone but commands enforcers.
11: ʾāmar bĕlibbô šākaḥ ʾēl (ʾāmar bĕlibbô šākaḥ ʾēl): 'he says in his heart, 'God has forgotten.'' The second inner monologue (cf. v.6) deepens the portrait of practical atheism: hissātîr pānāyw lōʾ-rāʾâ lānĕṣaḥ, 'he has hidden his face, he will never see.' The triple claim — God forgets, God hides, God never sees — mirrors an inversion of the divine assurances in Ps 9:12-13 (God remembers, God sees). The psalmist is not endorsing this view; he quotes the wicked to expose their delusion before calling on God to disprove it.
12: qûmâ YHWH ʾēl nĕśēʾ yādekā (qûmâ YHWH ʾēl nĕśēʾ yāḏeḵā): 'Arise, O LORD! O God, lift up your hand!' The liturgical imperative qûmâ returns from 9:19 — the lament section ends and the petition begins. The phrase ʾal-tiškaḥ ʿănāwîm, 'do not forget the afflicted,' directly refutes the wicked man's claim in v.11 (God has forgotten). The psalmist's prayer strategically uses the enemy's own language against him: if the wicked says 'God forgets,' the psalmist replies by praying 'do not forget.'
13: ʿal-mah niʾēṣ rāšāʿ ʾĕlōhîm (ʿal-mah niʾēṣ rāšāʿ ʾĕlōhîm): 'Why does the wicked despise God?' The niph'al niʾēṣ ('despise, treat with contempt') addresses divine honor at stake: YHWH's failure to act is the wicked man's theodicy in reverse — his impunity 'proves' God doesn't care. The wicked claims lōʾ tidrōš, 'you will not call to account,' treating divine patience as permanent indifference. The psalmist's complaint is partly apologetic: YHWH's honor requires that he vindicate the poor and disprove the wicked's theology.
14: rāʾîtâ kî-ʾattâ ʿāmāl wākaʿas tabbîṭ (rāʾîṯā ḵî-ʾattâ ʿāmāl wāḵaʿas tabbîṭ): 'You do see, for you observe trouble and vexation.' Against the wicked man's claim that God neither sees nor acts (v.11), the psalmist asserts rāʾîtâ — 'you have seen.' The infinitive lātet bĕyādekā, 'to give into your hand,' frames justice as a divine responsibility: the oppressed 'commits himself' (yaʿăzōb) to YHWH, and the fatherless (yātôm) finds his helper. The polarity of blindness (v.11) and sight (v.14) structures the entire psalm's theodicy.
15: šĕbōr zĕrôaʿ rāšāʿ wārāʿ (šĕḇôr zĕrôaʿ rāšāʿ wārāʿ): 'Break the arm of the wicked and evildoer.' The qal imperative šĕbōr ('break!') calls for decisive intervention: the arm (zĕrôaʿ) represents power — to break it is to disarm the oppressor. The phrase tidrōš rišʿô bĕl-timṣāʾ, 'seek out his wickedness until you find none,' envisions complete justice — not merely punishment but the full unraveling of wickedness until nothing remains. This is eschatological hope within an historical prayer.
16: YHWH melek ʿôlām wāʿed (YHWH melek ʿôlām wāʿeḏ): 'The LORD is king forever and ever.' The declaration of divine kingship (melek) forms the climax after the appeals: YHWH answers not as a distant judge but as a king whose rule is ʿôlām wāʿed ('age upon age'). The noun gôyim ('nations') appears again — āḇĕdû gôyim mēʾarṣô, 'the nations have perished from his land' — but now as past-tense historical fulfillment, not just eschatological hope. YHWH's land-claim has expelled the wicked.
17: tawat ʿănāwîm šāmaʿtâ YHWH (taʾăwaṯ ʿănāwîm šāmaʿtâ YHWH): 'The desire of the afflicted you have heard, O LORD.' The perfect šāmaʿtâ is a perfect of confidence: the psalmist speaks of future hearing as certain fact. The verb tākîn libbām ('you will establish their heart') suggests that divine attention strengthens the inner resolve of the afflicted — hearing transforms not just circumstances but persons. The outcome taqšîb ʾoznekā, 'you will incline your ear,' emphasizes receptive divine posture.
18: lišpōṭ yātôm wādāk (lišpōṭ yātôm wādāk): 'to do justice to the fatherless and the oppressed.' The infinitive lišpōṭ summarizes YHWH's purpose: to judge/vindicate. The pair yātôm wādāk ('fatherless and crushed') represent the most vulnerable — those with no human advocate. The final phrase bal-yôsip ʿôd laʿărōṣ ʾĕnôš min-hāʾāreṣ, 'that man of earth may strike terror no more,' returns to the ʾĕnôš ('mortal') of 9:20: the psalm's closing vision is the end of human tyranny before divine sovereignty. The earthly oppressor — whatever his momentary power — is finally, irreversibly exposed as merely mortal.
